column1: such as polymer molecular weight, lactide-to-glycolide ratio, and drug concentration to
achieve a desired dosage and release interval; also, end-capped functional groups are a key

3 of 13: In this comprehensive and systematic study, we successfully prepared PLGA NPs/MPs

column1: through a double emulsion solvent evaporation method to allow the successful and efﬁcient

3 of 13: encapsulation of PNA5 glycopeptide. Two types of poly(D,L-lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA)

column1: were used in the synthesis to produce nanoparticles and microparticles loaded with PNA5
(ester and acid-end capped). Comprehensive physicochemical characterization including

3 of 13: scanning electron microscopy, thermal analysis, molecular ﬁngerprinting spectroscopy,
particle sizing, drug loading, encapsulation efﬁciency, and in vitro drug release were con-

column1: ducted. To the authors’ knowledge, this is the ﬁrst systematic and comprehensive study to
report on the successful design, particle synthesis, physicochemical characterization, and
in vitro glycopeptide drug release of PNA5 PLGA nanoparticles and microparticles.
2. Materials and Methods
2.1. Materials

3 of 13: Various types of PLGA (50:50) diblock copolymers were purchased from Evonik (Essen,

column1: Polyvinyl Alcohol (PVA) (molecular weight 89,000-98,000 g/mol) was purchased from

column1: (HPLC grade) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Glycosylated

2.2. Methods
2.2.1. Preparation of PNA5 PLGA NPs/MPs Using Double Emulsion Solvent Evaporation

3 of 13: PLGA NPs and MPs loaded with PNA5 peptides were prepared by the double-
emulsiﬁcation solvent evaporation method (W1/O/W2), with two esters and acid ter-

column1: minated PLGA 50:50 as a polymeric system (R503 and R503H, respectively), conditions and

3 of 13: variables applied for the preparation of PNA5- micro/nanoparticles are shown in Table 1.

column1: Brieﬂy, PLGA was dissolved in dichloromethane (DCM), forming the oil phase (O), then
water phase in the ﬁrst emulsion (W1) was slowly added to the oil phase and sonicated at

3 of 13: 50% amplitude for 180 s (Sonics Vibra-Cell vcx-500, Sonics & Materials, Inc., Newtown, CT,

column1: USA) to get the ﬁrst emulsion (E1), then E1 was added to 2% w/v PVA solution (W2) to

3 of 13: form a second emulsion (E2), and an E2 was formed, either with homogenization (4500 rpm,

column1: Silverson L5M, Silverson Machines, Inc., East Longmeadow, MA, USA) or sonication (50%

3 of 13: amplitude for 60 s) for MP and NPs, respectively. Furthermore, for PLGA MPs synthesis,

column1: E2 was added to the W3 phase to solidify the microspheres. After 3 h of stirring (oil phase
solvent evaporation), MP and NPs were transferred to centrifuge tubes, washed three
times (4000 rpm, 10 min, 10,000 rpm, 20 min, respectively) with distilled water. Before the
freeze-drying process, particles were resuspended in a trehalose solution in a 1:2 weight
ratio (PLGA:Trehalose).
